text: Note that more advanced surgeons may choose to reduce the size of this incision.
However, this should only be attempted upon the consultant's request.
Next, the subcutaneous fat is divided along the midline with the electrocautery layer by layer to reach the periosteum.
To ensure you're in the midline prior to reaching the sternum, you will visualize the avascular plane between the muscles.
This is where you should divide down to the bone.
Of note, there is a small vein approximately 2 cm superior to the syphisternum which tends to bleed on entry.
This can be remedied by diathermy at this point to minimise blood loss.
Define the superior most border of the incision using the diathermy and cut around the jugular notch, taking care to remain on the bone at all times.
You can use a finger to retract the soft tissues and intermittently palpate the interclavicular ligament.
Then move to the inferior part of the incision and extend the soft tissue incision to the syphisternum.
Typically, a whole saw or an oscillating saw is used to divide the sternum.
Divide the sternal bone in one single upwards and forwards movement.
The diathermy can now be used for hemostasis around the periosteum of the sternum.
Some surgeons may also wish to use bone wax.
